_meta.authoringNotes: Ottawa Ankle Rules per Stiell et al. JAMA 1994 (PMID 8120512). Lateral ankle ligament injury epidemiology per Waterman et al. JBJS 2010 (PMID 21084581). Functional rehabilitation evidence per Kerkhoffs et al. Cochrane 2002 (PMID 12137620). Chronic ankle instability classification per Gribble et al. JOSPT 2013 (PMID 23898893).

h1Title: Ankle Sprain: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Ankle sprain is the most common musculoskeletal injury in active populations, with US emergency departments treating approximately 2 million cases per year (Waterman 2010). The lateral ligament complex — anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL), calcaneofibular ligament (CFL), and posterior talofibular ligament (PTFL) — is involved in 85% of sprains, typically through an inversion mechanism when the foot rolls inward. Pain, swelling, and ecchymosis on the lateral side of the ankle follow within hours. The Ottawa Ankle Rules accurately identify the 15% of patients needing X-rays to exclude fracture, sparing the rest unnecessary imaging. Functional rehabilitation with early controlled motion, balance training, and progressive return to sport recovers 70-85% of athletes fully within 6-8 weeks; 20-30% develop chronic ankle instability requiring sustained rehabilitation or surgery.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 2 million US emergency department visits annually (Waterman 2010); incidence roughly 2-7 per 1,000 person-years
keyStats.demographics: Highest rates in young adults aged 15-24; basketball, football, soccer, and running account for over half of sports-related cases
keyStats.avgAge: Peak age 15-19 in athletes; bimodal distribution with second peak in older adults from falls
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated tens of millions of ankle sprains globally each year; under-reported because most are managed at home or in primary care

definition: Ankle sprain (ICD-10: S93.4 sprain of ligaments of ankle) is an acute injury to one or more of the ankle ligaments produced by sudden mechanical loading beyond the ligament's tensile capacity. The most common form is the lateral (inversion) ankle sprain affecting the lateral ligament complex: anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL, injured in roughly 65-85% of cases), calcaneofibular ligament (CFL, 50-75%), and posterior talofibular ligament (PTFL, less than 10%). The medial (deltoid) ligament is injured less commonly through eversion mechanisms. High ankle sprains involve the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis (anterior inferior tibiofibular ligament and interosseous membrane) and typically result from forced external rotation in a fixed foot — a mechanism common in football, hockey, and skiing. Sprains are graded by severity: grade 1 (microscopic fiber damage, no instability), grade 2 (partial tear with mild instability), and grade 3 (complete ligament rupture with marked instability). Approximately 20-30% of patients develop chronic ankle instability with recurrent giving-way, persistent pain, and functional impairment — a complication strongly associated with insufficient initial rehabilitation. The condition is managed by primary care doctors, podiatrists, sports medicine physicians, emergency physicians, and physiotherapists. Complex or recurrent cases are seen by foot and ankle orthopedic surgeons.

typesClassification.description: Most common form (85% of all ankle sprains). Foot rolls inward while planted, stretching or tearing the lateral ligaments (ATFL, CFL, PTFL). Pain and swelling concentrated over the lateral malleolus.
Less common (5-15%). Foot rolls outward, stretching or tearing the deltoid ligament. Often associated with fibular fracture or syndesmotic injury and warrants more careful evaluation.

Injury of the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis. Forced external rotation in a planted foot. Pain superior to the lateral malleolus along the syndesmosis; squeeze and external rotation stress tests positive. Recovery typically 2-3x longer than lateral sprain.
Microscopic ligament fiber damage. No mechanical instability on examination. Mild pain and swelling. Athletes return to sport within 1-2 weeks.
Partial ligament tear with mild to moderate instability on examination. Significant pain, swelling, ecchymosis. Return to sport 3-6 weeks.
Complete ligament rupture with marked instability. Inability to bear weight, significant ecchymosis. Return to sport 6-12 weeks; some require surgical repair.

primarySymptoms: Sudden pain on the outer or inner side of the ankle immediately after a twisting injury during sport, walking on uneven ground, or a fall.
Audible or palpable pop at the moment of injury in moderate to severe sprains.
Localized tenderness directly over the lateral malleolus, the ATFL, the CFL, or the deltoid ligament depending on the mechanism.
Swelling that develops within minutes to a few hours, often disproportionate to the apparent severity of injury.
Ecchymosis (bruising) along the lateral or medial ankle and tracking distally to the foot over 24-72 hours.
Inability to bear full weight immediately after the injury in moderate or severe sprains.
Stiffness and reduced ankle range of motion, particularly inversion and dorsiflexion.
Sensation of the ankle giving way, especially on uneven surfaces or stairs, in recurrent or severe cases.
Tenderness along the medial malleolus, base of the fifth metatarsal, navicular, or proximal fibula — important sites to assess for fracture per the Ottawa Ankle Rules.
Persistent pain on functional activities (running, jumping, cutting) for weeks to months in poorly rehabilitated sprains.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild ankle pain after a minor twisting injury that worsens over hours
Brief 'giving way' sensation during sport followed by full recovery within minutes
Recurrent mild ankle sprains in someone with no history of severe injury — early chronic ankle instability
Reduced single-leg balance on the affected side compared with the contralateral side
Subtle ankle stiffness in the morning that resolves with activity in chronic instability
emergencySigns: Inability to bear weight on the injured ankle for four steps in the emergency department and immediately after the injury — apply Ottawa Ankle Rules and obtain radiographs
Bony tenderness over the posterior edge or tip of either malleolus, the navicular, or the base of the fifth metatarsal — Ottawa Ankle Rules indication for X-ray
Severe deformity, neurovascular compromise, or open injury — emergency department evaluation
Numbness, tingling, or color change in the foot — exclude nerve or vascular injury
Severe disproportionate pain with calf tenderness and swelling after immobilization — exclude deep vein thrombosis

causes.description: The most common mechanism (85% of sprains). The foot rolls inward while planted, often when landing from a jump on uneven ground, stepping off a curb, or being stepped on by another athlete. The ATFL is the first ligament to fail; with greater force the CFL and then PTFL follow.

causes.cause: Forced external rotation with planted foot (high ankle sprain)
causes.description: Common in football, hockey, and skiing when the foot is fixed and the body rotates outward. The distal tibiofibular syndesmosis is stretched or torn. Recovery is significantly longer than lateral sprains.
Less common; the foot rolls outward stretching the deltoid ligament. Often associated with fibular fracture or syndesmotic injury. Requires careful clinical and radiographic evaluation.

causes.cause: Recurrent injury and chronic ankle instability
causes.description: Up to 30% of first-time ankle sprains progress to chronic ankle instability. Mechanical instability (residual ligament laxity) and functional instability (impaired proprioception, neuromuscular control) both contribute. Inadequate initial rehabilitation is the strongest predictor.
High-heeled shoes, unsupportive footwear, and walking or running on uneven ground all raise the risk of inversion injury. Sport-specific footwear reduces incidence in basketball and indoor sports.

Cavus (high-arched) foot type, hindfoot varus, reduced ankle dorsiflexion, and proprioceptive deficits all raise inversion injury risk. Athletes with prior sprains carry markedly increased risk on subsequent loading.

riskFactors.description: Single biggest predictor of future sprain — recurrence risk roughly 2-3x higher than baseline. Driven by residual ligament laxity, proprioceptive deficit, and altered neuromuscular control after the index injury.
Basketball, football, soccer, volleyball, and racquet sports show the highest incidence. Up to half of all basketball injuries are ankle sprains.

riskFactors.factor: Female sex in basketball and similar sports
riskFactors.description: Female athletes have slightly higher incidence in cutting and jumping sports, partly because of biomechanical and hormonal factors. The difference is smaller than for ACL injuries.
High-arched feet predispose to inversion through reduced subtalar mobility and altered ground contact. Hindfoot varus alignment compounds the risk.
Limited ankle dorsiflexion alters landing mechanics and raises injury risk. Calf and ankle mobility programs reduce sprain incidence in athletes.

Reduced single-leg balance on the affected side predicts both initial and recurrent sprains. Balance and proprioception training (including wobble boards and BOSU) reduces recurrence by approximately 35-50%.
Non-sport-specific shoes, worn footwear, and high heels raise inversion injury risk. Court shoes provide better lateral support than running shoes for cutting sports.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 2 million ankle sprains per year in the US (Waterman et al. JBJS 2010).
Highest incidence in active adults aged 15-24, with basketball, football, and soccer accounting for over half of sports-related cases.
Female adolescents have slightly higher rates than male peers in basketball and other cutting sports.
Up to 30% of first-time sprains progress to chronic ankle instability with recurrent sprains and functional limitation.
Recurrent ankle sprains are a leading cause of long-term ankle osteoarthritis in survivors of multiple injuries.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical. The history establishes the mechanism (inversion, eversion, external rotation), audible pop, immediate weight-bearing ability, prior ankle injuries, and footwear. Examination follows a structured approach: inspection for swelling, ecchymosis, and deformity; palpation along the lateral and medial malleoli, ATFL, CFL, deltoid ligament, syndesmosis, base of fifth metatarsal, navicular, and Achilles tendon; range of motion in dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, inversion, and eversion; and special tests including the anterior drawer test (ATFL integrity), talar tilt test (CFL integrity), squeeze test (syndesmosis), and external rotation test (syndesmosis). The Ottawa Ankle Rules identify the 15% of patients needing radiographs: bone tenderness at the posterior edge or tip of either malleolus, bone tenderness at the navicular or base of the fifth metatarsal, or inability to bear weight for four steps both immediately after injury and in the emergency department. Three-view ankle radiographs (AP, lateral, mortise) and dedicated foot radiographs (when foot tenderness is present) detect most fractures. MRI is reserved for suspected osteochondral injury, persistent symptoms past 6-8 weeks, suspected high ankle sprain not responding to standard care, or pre-operative planning. Ultrasound, increasingly used at the point of care, demonstrates ligament tears, hematoma, and dynamic instability. In chronic ankle instability, stress radiographs (Telos device) may quantify mechanical laxity but are less commonly used in modern practice.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the 15% of patients who need radiographs to exclude fracture; sensitivity 98-100% for clinically significant fractures
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10 minute focused examination. The clinician palpates the posterior edge and tip of each malleolus, the navicular, and the base of the fifth metatarsal, and asks whether the patient could bear weight for four steps. Negative result safely excludes the need for X-ray.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses ATFL integrity; positive when the talus translates anteriorly more than 5 mm or significantly more than the contralateral side
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient seated with knee flexed. Examiner stabilizes the tibia and pulls the heel anteriorly. Comparison with the uninjured side is essential. Quick, painless test if the injury is more than 48 hours old.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses combined ATFL and CFL integrity; positive when inversion produces visible joint opening or excessive talar tilt compared with the other side
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed with patient seated and ankle in neutral. Examiner inverts the heel and observes joint opening. Best assessed several days after acute injury when guarding has subsided.

diagnosticTests.test: Squeeze test and external rotation stress test (syndesmosis)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify high ankle sprain involving the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Squeeze test: examiner squeezes the proximal tibia and fibula together; pain at the distal syndesmosis is positive. External rotation: ankle in neutral, examiner externally rotates the foot; pain at the syndesmosis is positive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes fracture when Ottawa Ankle Rules positive; assesses mortise alignment and joint space
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard radiographs taken within 30 minutes in the emergency department. Low radiation dose. Reading by radiologist or emergency physician trained in musculoskeletal imaging.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates osteochondral lesions, ligament tears, syndesmotic injury, and other soft tissue pathology when symptoms persist past 6-8 weeks
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes lying still in the scanner. No contrast required unless arthrogram. Reports grade ligament injury and identify chondral defects of the talar dome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Real-time assessment of ligament integrity, hematoma, and dynamic instability; increasingly available at point of care
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies supine with knee flexed. Sonographer applies gel and a linear-array probe. 15-20 minutes; no ionizing radiation. Skilled operator dependent.

treatmentOverview: Modern management of ankle sprain has shifted from prolonged immobilization to early functional rehabilitation. The acute phase (first 72 hours) follows the POLICE principle (Protection, Optimal Loading, Ice, Compression, Elevation): use of a removable brace or stirrup splint for protection, weight-bearing as tolerated rather than complete non-weight-bearing, ice 10-15 minutes every 2-3 hours, compression with an elastic bandage, and elevation when seated. Crutches are used briefly if antalgic gait is unavoidable. NSAIDs for 3-5 days reduce pain and swelling in acute sprains; long-term use is avoided. Early controlled mobilization within pain-free range starts on day 1-2. Functional rehabilitation begins by week 1: range of motion, then progressive strengthening of peroneal, posterior tibial, and intrinsic foot muscles, balance and proprioception training (wobble board, BOSU, single-leg balance), and sport-specific drills. The evidence overwhelmingly supports functional rehabilitation over rigid casting; Cochrane meta-analyses show better long-term outcomes and faster return to sport. Return-to-sport is guided by functional criteria rather than time alone: full pain-free range of motion, near-normal peroneal strength, single-leg balance equal to the uninjured side, ability to hop, cut, and pivot without giving way, and clinician clearance. Average return-to-play: grade 1, 7-14 days; grade 2, 14-28 days; grade 3, 4-8 weeks; high ankle sprain, 6-12 weeks or longer. Persistent symptoms past 6-8 weeks despite optimal care warrant imaging and specialist evaluation. Chronic ankle instability with recurrent sprains and giving-way may require continued rehabilitation, ankle braces during sport, and in selected cases surgical ligament reconstruction (Brostrom procedure with or without internal brace augmentation). Bracing during high-risk sport reduces recurrence in athletes with prior sprains by approximately 40-50%.

medicalTreatments.name: POLICE protocol (first 72 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Protection with removable brace, optimal loading (weight-bearing within pain tolerance), ice 10-15 minutes every 2-3 hours, compression bandage, elevation. Replaces older PRICE/RICE protocols by emphasizing early gentle loading.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces early pain and swelling; faster return to functional activities than rigid immobilization

medicalTreatments.name: Functional rehabilitation with structured physiotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Progressive range of motion, peroneal and intrinsic foot strengthening, balance and proprioception training, sport-specific drills over 4-8 weeks. The cornerstone of modern ankle sprain management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Faster return to sport and lower recurrence than immobilization; reduces chronic ankle instability risk by approximately 50% (Cochrane evidence)

medicalTreatments.name: Removable ankle brace (lace-up or stirrup)
medicalTreatments.description: Provides external support during the acute phase and during return to sport. Lace-up braces are typically more supportive than stirrups but less rigid than walking boots. Worn during sport for 6-12 months after a significant sprain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrence by approximately 40-50% in athletes with prior sprains; allows early functional rehabilitation

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course NSAIDs (3-5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily or naproxen 500 mg twice daily for 3-5 days reduces acute pain and swelling. Topical NSAIDs are an alternative with less systemic exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful pain and swelling reduction in 60-75% of patients

medicalTreatments.name: Balance and proprioception training (wobble board, BOSU, single-leg)
medicalTreatments.description: Targets the proprioceptive deficits that drive recurrent sprains. Programs of 6-12 weeks reduce recurrence by 35-50% in randomized trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recurrence reduction in athletes with prior ankle sprain; valuable as both rehabilitation and prevention

medicalTreatments.name: Walking boot for severe sprains (grade 3, syndesmotic)
medicalTreatments.description: Removable walking boot or stirrup brace for the first 1-2 weeks in severe lateral sprains and 4-6 weeks in high ankle sprains. Allows progressive weight-bearing while protecting healing ligaments.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective protection during the early phase of severe sprains; allows early ambulation compared with non-weight-bearing casting

surgicalOptions.name: Brostrom-Gould lateral ligament repair
surgicalOptions.description: Direct repair of the ATFL and CFL with imbrication and reinforcement using the inferior extensor retinaculum (Gould modification). Performed for chronic lateral ankle instability after failed rehabilitation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Return to sport and stability in 80-90% of selected patients; recurrence under 10% at 5-10 years

surgicalOptions.name: Brostrom with internal brace augmentation
surgicalOptions.description: Anatomic ligament repair augmented with a suture-tape internal brace. Allows earlier accelerated rehabilitation and earlier return to sport than traditional Brostrom alone.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Return to sport in 85-95% in published series; allows earlier rehabilitation than open Brostrom alone

surgicalOptions.name: Syndesmotic screw or TightRope fixation
surgicalOptions.description: Internal fixation of unstable high ankle sprains, typically when the syndesmosis remains unstable on stress imaging or there is significant diastasis. TightRope (suture button) increasingly preferred over rigid screws.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restoration of syndesmotic alignment in over 90%; suture button has lower rates of hardware removal compared with screws

surgicalOptions.name: Ankle arthroscopy for osteochondral lesion
surgicalOptions.description: Performed for cartilage and osteochondral lesions of the talar dome that develop after acute sprain or chronic instability. Microfracture or chondroplasty depending on lesion size.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70-85% for small lesions; larger lesions may need autologous chondrocyte implantation

alternativeTreatments.name: Manual therapy and joint mobilization
alternativeTreatments.description: Therapist-applied joint mobilization may improve dorsiflexion and reduce stiffness in the acute and subacute phases. Best used alongside functional rehabilitation rather than as standalone therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Comprehensive return-to-sport progression with criteria-based clearance
alternativeTreatments.description: Functional return-to-sport tests (hop tests, side-to-side jumps, sport-specific drills) replace time-based clearance and reduce reinjury when meticulously followed.

whySeeSpecialist: Podiatric, sports medicine, or orthopedic referral is appropriate for severe (grade 3) sprains, syndesmotic injury, persistent symptoms past 6-8 weeks, recurrent sprains, chronic ankle instability, or suspected osteochondral injury. Specialists confirm diagnosis with ultrasound or MRI, structure return-to-sport progression, and consider surgery in refractory chronic instability.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a podiatrist or foot and ankle specialist experienced in functional rehabilitation, chronic ankle instability, and surgical reconstruction when indicated. Centers offering same-day ultrasound, MRI access, sports physiotherapy familiar with proprioception training, and outpatient ligament reconstruction provide the most complete care.
hospitalCriteria: Emergency department able to apply Ottawa Ankle Rules and provide radiographs when needed
Sports physiotherapy team experienced in functional rehabilitation and balance training
Musculoskeletal ultrasound and MRI access for persistent or complex cases
Foot and ankle orthopedic surgical service for chronic instability or osteochondral lesions
Bracing and orthotic services for return-to-sport support
keyFacilities: Emergency departments and urgent care centers
Sports medicine clinics with same-day imaging
preventionStrategies: Continue balance and proprioception training (wobble board, single-leg stance) two to three times per week, especially after a previous sprain.
Wear sport-specific footwear in good condition; replace worn athletic shoes every 6-12 months.
Use an ankle brace during high-risk sports (basketball, volleyball, soccer) for 6-12 months after a significant sprain.
Maintain ankle dorsiflexion mobility with daily calf and ankle stretches.
Strengthen peroneal, tibialis posterior, and intrinsic foot muscles as part of routine training.
Use the FIFA 11+ warm-up or equivalent neuromuscular training programs that reduce lower-limb injuries by 30-40% in cohort studies.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid premature return to high-risk activity before completing functional rehabilitation criteria.
Wear a lace-up brace during cutting and jumping sport for at least 6 months after a significant sprain.
Inspect playing surfaces for uneven terrain and remove tripping hazards at home.
Maintain a sleep schedule and adequate recovery between training sessions.
Track ankle stability and any episodes of giving-way; report to clinician if recurrent.
Address foot type (cavus, hindfoot varus) with appropriate footwear and orthoses where indicated.

recommended: Adequate protein (1.0-1.4 g/kg/day) to support soft-tissue and ligament healing
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers, berries) supporting collagen synthesis
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods for bone health during reduced weight-bearing
Anti-inflammatory whole foods (oily fish, vegetables, fruit, olive oil)
avoid: Excessive alcohol — impairs balance and increases re-injury risk during recovery
Tobacco use — impairs ligament and soft-tissue healing
Long-term high-dose NSAIDs during the healing phase — theoretical impairment of soft-tissue repair
Excessive caffeine intake that disrupts sleep and recovery

exerciseGuidelines: Days 0-3: weight-bearing as tolerated, gentle range of motion (alphabet writing), seated dorsiflexion stretches. Days 3-14: progressive range of motion, theraband peroneal strengthening, double-leg balance, stationary cycling. Weeks 2-4: single-leg balance, wobble board, BOSU, progressive sport-specific drills. Weeks 4-8: hopping, cutting, sport-specific reintroduction. Return-to-sport based on functional criteria, not time alone.
dailyManagement: Ice the ankle for 10-15 minutes every 2-3 hours during the first 48-72 hours.
Wear a compression bandage or brace during waking hours in the first 1-2 weeks.
Elevate the foot above heart level when seated or sleeping for the first 3-5 days.
Perform the prescribed rehabilitation exercises on alternate days.
Wear lace-up brace during sport for at least 6 months after a significant sprain.
Track giving-way episodes and report any to clinician at follow-up.
prognosis: Most ankle sprains have excellent outcomes with appropriate functional rehabilitation. Grade 1 sprains: full return to sport in 1-2 weeks, recurrence 5-15%. Grade 2: 2-6 weeks, recurrence 15-25%. Grade 3: 6-12 weeks, recurrence 25-35% without comprehensive rehabilitation. High ankle sprains: 6-12 weeks or longer with potential for chronic syndesmotic pain. Approximately 20-30% of all sprains progress to chronic ankle instability with recurrent sprains, persistent pain, and giving-way — strongly associated with inadequate initial rehabilitation. Predictors of poor outcome are prior sprain, persistent strength or balance deficit at return-to-sport, untreated osteochondral injury, and a high-volume cutting or jumping sport. Surgical lateral ligament reconstruction (Brostrom procedure) restores 80-90% of selected patients to sport. Long-term, multiple severe sprains predispose to ankle osteoarthritis — an additional reason for comprehensive initial rehabilitation.
recoveryTimeline: Grade 1: pain settles within 5-7 days; full return to sport at 1-2 weeks. Grade 2: pain resolves over 2-3 weeks; full sport at 4-6 weeks. Grade 3: walking pain-free by 3-4 weeks; full sport at 8-12 weeks. High ankle sprain: walking by 3-6 weeks; full sport at 8-16 weeks or longer. Chronic ankle instability: progressive improvement with rehabilitation over 12-24 weeks.
complications: Chronic ankle instability with recurrent sprains and giving-way in 20-30% of cases, more common after inadequate initial rehabilitation.
Osteochondral lesion of the talar dome, presenting with deep aching ankle pain and clicking; identified on MRI and treatable with arthroscopic procedures.
Peroneal tendon pathology (tear, subluxation) — common after lateral ankle sprain and often missed initially; ultrasound or MRI confirms.
Subtalar joint instability — under-recognized cause of persistent ankle and hindfoot pain after sprain.
Anterolateral impingement from scar tissue or fibrosis in the lateral gutter; responds to corticosteroid injection or arthroscopic debridement.
Long-term ankle osteoarthritis in patients with multiple severe sprains and chronic instability.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Sprained Ankle
supportResources.description: Patient-friendly guide to diagnosis, treatment, and return to sport for ankle sprains.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Foot & Ankle Society (AOFAS)
supportResources.description: Patient-facing condition pages on ankle sprain and chronic ankle instability with find-a-surgeon directory.

supportResources.name: American Medical Society for Sports Medicine (AMSSM)
supportResources.description: US sports medicine professional society with patient resources on ankle sprain management and return to sport.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association (APMA) — Ankle Sprain
supportResources.description: US podiatric professional society offering condition pages on ankle sprain and chronic ankle instability.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stress Fracture
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Stress fracture causes gradually worsening pain over a specific bony site without a single traumatic event. Ankle sprain produces sudden pain with a clear injury mechanism. The Ottawa Ankle Rules identify the bony tenderness sites where stress fractures (and acute fractures) need imaging.

confusedWithConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achilles tendinitis causes pain and tenderness over the tendon 2-6 cm above the calcaneus, with gradual onset during running. Ankle sprain is acute, with lateral or medial ligament tenderness and a clear twisting mechanism. The two affect different anatomic structures.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis causes plantar heel pain, especially with first steps in the morning. Ankle sprain causes lateral or medial ankle pain with a twisting mechanism. The pain location and onset distinguish them clearly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bunion (Hallux Valgus)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bunion is a chronic forefoot deformity of the great toe MTP joint. Ankle sprain is an acute injury of the ankle ligaments. Both can occur together in older adults with multiple foot conditions but are unrelated.

relatedConditions.name: Shin Splints
relatedConditions.relevance: Common overuse injury in running and cutting sport athletes who also sustain ankle sprains; both respond to load management and structured rehabilitation.

relatedConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Adjacent overuse injury that may coexist with ankle sprain in the same athlete; shares calf-stretching and eccentric loading rehabilitation principles.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Common foot condition that overlaps with ankle sprain populations; both managed by podiatry and sports medicine.

relatedConditions.name: Hamstring Strain
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common acute musculoskeletal injury in cutting and sprinting sports; shares principles of progressive loading rehabilitation and return-to-sport criteria.

relatedConditions.name: Runner's Knee (Patellofemoral Syndrome)
relatedConditions.relevance: Common cumulative load injury in athletes who experience recurrent ankle sprains; hip and proximal strength contribute to both.

faqs.question: What is an ankle sprain?
faqs.answer: An ankle sprain is an injury to one or more of the ligaments that stabilize the ankle, most commonly the lateral ligaments (ATFL, CFL, PTFL). It typically results from rolling the foot inward, causing immediate pain, swelling, and reduced ability to bear weight on the outer side of the ankle.

faqs.question: How long does an ankle sprain take to heal?
faqs.answer: Grade 1 sprains heal in 1-2 weeks. Grade 2 sprains take 2-6 weeks. Grade 3 sprains need 6-12 weeks with structured rehabilitation. High ankle (syndesmotic) sprains take 6-12 weeks or longer. Return to full sport is decided by functional testing rather than time alone.

faqs.question: Do I need an X-ray for my ankle sprain?
faqs.answer: Apply the Ottawa Ankle Rules: an X-ray is needed if you cannot bear weight for four steps immediately after the injury and in the emergency department, or if there is bone tenderness over the malleoli, navicular, or base of the fifth metatarsal. About 85% of sprains do not need imaging.

faqs.question: Should I walk on a sprained ankle?
faqs.answer: Walk within pain tolerance from day one — complete non-weight-bearing slows recovery. Use a brace or stirrup splint to protect the ankle. Crutches are used only if walking is severely painful. Early controlled weight-bearing is now the standard of care.

faqs.question: How do I know if my ankle is broken or sprained?
faqs.answer: A fracture typically causes severe focal bone pain, inability to bear weight, and tenderness over a specific bony site (malleolus, fifth metatarsal, navicular). A sprain causes ligament tenderness and is often able to bear partial weight after a few minutes. The Ottawa Ankle Rules and an X-ray when indicated distinguish them.

faqs.question: What exercises help an ankle sprain?
faqs.answer: Range-of-motion exercises (alphabet writing, ankle pumps), peroneal strengthening with resistance bands, calf stretching, single-leg balance, wobble board and BOSU training, and progressive sport-specific drills form the rehabilitation core. A physiotherapist guides progression over 4-8 weeks.

faqs.question: When can I return to sport after an ankle sprain?
faqs.answer: Return to sport is guided by functional criteria: full pain-free range of motion, near-normal peroneal strength, single-leg balance equal to the uninjured side, ability to hop, cut, and pivot without pain or giving way, and clinician clearance. Typical timelines: grade 1, 1-2 weeks; grade 2, 3-6 weeks; grade 3, 6-12 weeks.

faqs.question: Should I ice an ankle sprain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Apply ice for 10-15 minutes every 2-3 hours during the first 48-72 hours. Wrap ice in a thin towel to avoid direct skin contact. After the first 72 hours the benefit of icing decreases and progressive loading becomes more important.

faqs.question: Can a sprained ankle heal without treatment?
faqs.answer: Mild grade 1 sprains often improve without medical care, but inadequately rehabilitated sprains carry a 20-30% risk of chronic ankle instability. Even mild sprains benefit from structured rehabilitation including balance training, which reduces recurrence by 35-50%.

faqs.question: What is a high ankle sprain?
faqs.answer: A high ankle sprain involves the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis above the ankle joint, usually from forced external rotation in a fixed foot. Pain is located higher than the lateral malleolus. Recovery is significantly longer than lateral ankle sprains, typically 6-12 weeks or more.

faqs.question: Can ankle sprains recur?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to 30% of first-time sprains progress to chronic ankle instability with recurrent sprains and giving-way. The strongest predictor of recurrence is inadequate initial rehabilitation. Comprehensive proprioception training and ankle bracing during sport reduce recurrence substantially.

faqs.question: Do ankle braces help prevent sprains?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lace-up braces worn during high-risk sports reduce recurrent sprains by approximately 40-50% in athletes with prior injury. They are recommended for at least 6 months after a significant sprain and during high-risk cutting and jumping activity.

faqs.question: What is the difference between sprain and strain?
faqs.answer: A sprain is an injury to a ligament (the tissue connecting bones). A strain is an injury to a muscle or tendon (the tissue connecting muscle to bone). Ankle sprains involve the ankle ligaments; calf and Achilles injuries are usually strains. Treatment principles overlap but specific rehabilitation differs.

faqs.question: Can children get ankle sprains?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children and adolescents commonly sprain ankles in sports and play. Skeletal immaturity sometimes leads to growth-plate fractures rather than pure ligament sprains, particularly in children under 12. Pediatric ankle injuries with significant swelling or persistent pain warrant X-rays.

faqs.question: Is surgery ever needed for an ankle sprain?
faqs.answer: Surgery is reserved for chronic ankle instability after 6+ months of failed rehabilitation, unstable syndesmotic injuries, and significant osteochondral lesions. The Brostrom procedure restores lateral ankle stability in 80-90% of selected patients. Over 95% of acute sprains recover fully without surgery.

faqs.question: How can I prevent ankle sprains?
faqs.answer: Maintain balance and proprioception training (wobble board, single-leg stance) two to three times per week. Wear sport-specific footwear in good condition. Use a lace-up brace during high-risk sports if you have a prior sprain. Maintain ankle dorsiflexion mobility and peroneal strength. Use FIFA 11+ or similar neuromuscular warm-up programs.

faqs.question: Why does my ankle keep rolling after a sprain?
faqs.answer: Recurrent giving-way reflects chronic ankle instability — a combination of residual ligament laxity and impaired proprioception and neuromuscular control. Comprehensive rehabilitation focused on balance and peroneal strengthening typically resolves the problem; surgical reconstruction is reserved for refractory cases.

faqs.question: Can ankle sprains cause arthritis later in life?
faqs.answer: Multiple severe sprains and chronic ankle instability can predispose to long-term ankle osteoarthritis through cartilage damage and altered joint mechanics. A single well-rehabilitated sprain has minimal long-term risk. Comprehensive rehabilitation and bracing after significant sprains reduce this risk.

faqs.question: Should I use a walking boot for an ankle sprain?
faqs.answer: A walking boot is used for severe (grade 3) lateral sprains for the first 1-2 weeks and for high ankle sprains for 4-6 weeks. It allows protected weight-bearing while ligaments heal. Most grade 1 and 2 sprains are better managed with a removable brace and early functional rehabilitation.

faqs.question: Are NSAIDs safe for ankle sprains?
faqs.answer: Short-course NSAIDs (3-5 days) are reasonable for acute pain and swelling and have not been shown to slow recovery in clinical trials. Long-term high-dose use is discouraged because of theoretical interference with soft-tissue healing. Paracetamol and topical NSAIDs are alternatives with less systemic exposure.

faqs.question: Can I sprain my ankle in non-sport activities?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stepping off a curb, walking on uneven ground, slipping on ice, and falling on stairs are common non-sport causes. Older adults are particularly prone to sprains from falls and uneven surfaces. The rehabilitation and prevention principles apply equally to sport and non-sport sprains.
